title: Fall Prevention & Safe Patient Handling
subtitle: Basic Nursing Care · keeping clients safe from injury
scenario: Preventing falls and moving clients safely on a behavioral-health unit.

steps.q: Which client factor represents the HIGHEST risk for a fall?

opts.text: A history of a recent fall
opts.rat: A prior recent fall is one of the strongest predictors of another fall.

opts.text: Wearing eyeglasses
opts.rat: Corrected vision generally reduces, not increases, fall risk.

opts.text: Being oriented to person, place, and time
opts.rat: Orientation lowers risk compared with confusion.

opts.text: Sleeping 8 hours a night
opts.rat: Adequate sleep is not a fall-risk factor.

steps.q: Which environmental measure best reduces a client’s fall risk?

opts.text: Raising all four side rails on the bed
opts.rat: Four raised rails can be a restraint and may increase injury if the client climbs over.

opts.text: Keeping the bed in a low, locked position with the call light in reach
opts.rat: A low, locked bed and an accessible call light are core fall-prevention measures.

opts.text: Dimming all lights to help the client rest
opts.rat: Poor lighting increases fall risk; a nightlight is safer.

opts.text: Placing personal items across the room to encourage walking
opts.rat: Out-of-reach items tempt unsafe reaching and ambulation.

steps.q: Using proper body mechanics when assisting a client, the technician should:

opts.text: Bend at the waist and keep the legs straight
opts.rat: Bending at the waist strains the back.

opts.text: Bend at the knees and hips, keep the load close, and avoid twisting
opts.rat: Using the legs, keeping the load close, and pivoting the feet protect the technician’s back.

opts.text: Twist at the trunk to move the client quickly
opts.rat: Twisting the trunk under load is a common cause of back injury.

opts.text: Hold the client as far from the body as possible
opts.rat: Holding a load away from the body increases strain.

steps.q: A client becomes dizzy and begins to fall while walking with the technician. The BEST action is to:

opts.text: Grab the client’s arm and hold them upright
opts.rat: Trying to hold a falling adult fully upright can injure both people.

opts.text: Ease the client to the floor while protecting the head, widening your stance
opts.rat: Controlled lowering with a wide base of support and head protection is the safest response to an in-progress fall.

opts.text: Step away so the client does not pull you down
opts.rat: Stepping away leaves the client unprotected.

opts.text: Try to catch the client’s full weight
opts.rat: Catching full weight risks serious injury to the technician and client.

steps.q: Physical restraints as a fall-prevention measure should be:

opts.text: Applied routinely to any client at risk of falling
opts.rat: Routine restraint is never appropriate and can cause harm.

opts.text: A last resort, requiring an order and the least-restrictive option
opts.rat: Restraints are a last resort, require a provider order, must be least-restrictive, and are monitored closely.

opts.text: Left in place for the entire shift without checks
opts.rat: Restrained clients require frequent monitoring and release per policy.

opts.text: Used whenever the unit is short-staffed
opts.rat: Staffing is never a lawful reason to restrain a client.

steps.q: Immediately after a client falls, the technician should FIRST:

opts.text: Help the client back into bed right away
opts.rat: Moving the client before assessing could worsen an injury.

opts.text: Assess the client for injury and check vital signs
opts.rat: Assess for injury and obtain vital signs first, then notify the nurse and document/complete an incident report.

opts.text: Complete the incident report before anything else
opts.rat: Client assessment comes before paperwork.

opts.text: Clean the area so no one else falls
opts.rat: Client safety assessment takes priority over housekeeping.

steps.q: To reduce falls, clients should be encouraged to wear:

opts.text: Loose slippers without backs
opts.rat: Backless, loose slippers slide off and increase falls.

opts.text: Non-slip footwear that fits securely
opts.rat: Well-fitting, non-slip footwear improves stability and reduces falls.

opts.text: Socks only on smooth floors
opts.rat: Plain socks are slippery on hard flooring.

opts.text: Sandals with a raised heel
opts.rat: Raised heels and open sandals reduce stability.

steps.q: When transferring a weak client from the bed to a chair, the technician should:

opts.text: Pull the client up by one arm
opts.rat: Pulling on one arm can dislocate the shoulder and is unsafe.

opts.text: Use a gait/transfer belt and lock the wheels of the bed and chair
opts.rat: A transfer belt plus locked wheels provides a secure, controlled transfer.

opts.text: Have the client hold onto the IV pole for support
opts.rat: IV poles roll and cannot bear weight safely.

opts.text: Transfer quickly before the client tires
opts.rat: Rushing a transfer increases the risk of a fall.
